text: Highlight Clip Michael Saylor: Does not rule out Strategy selling some Bitcoin by year end On May 21, 2026, Strategy Executive Chairman Michael Saylor responded to market speculation regarding potential Bitcoin sell-offs during an interview on Natalie Brunell's show. He stated that the company takes a matter-of-fact approach to its asset management. Saylor believes that to achieve the ultimate goal of maximizing bitcoin per share by 2033, the company will flexibly allocate assets, which may include a mixed strategy of selling equity and credit instruments, as well as managing USD and cash reserves. Consequently, he acknowledged that he does not rule out the possibility of selling some bitcoin before the end of the year. — link

text: Vitalik shares his personal perspective on the Ethereum Foundation's ongoing transition • Emphasizes its shift from a central role to one specialized node focused on longevity, limited resources, and activities uniquely critical for Ethereum's censorship resistance, openness, privacy, and security (CROPS) • Highlights the EF's reduced ETH holdings at 0.16%, completed original mandate, and strategic choices to support external efforts, making hard decisions on scope while opinionated stands ensure tasks attract outside capital and maintain ecosystem pluralism • Core technical vision centers on making Ethereum "deeply impressive" through provably bug-free code via AI formal verification, hybrid consensus resilience, and intermediary minimization like FOCIL, all compatible with scaling and high TPS to strengthen ETH as an asset

text: Learn mechanistic interpretability Mechanistic interpretability is about opening up neural networks and reverse-engineering what they are actually doing. Instead of treating model weights as random noise, the curriculum shows that models often learn readable structures, features, circuits, and even clean algorithms The six projects build from simple to frontier-level interpretability. You start by visualising digit-classifier weights, then learn superposition, grokking, induction heads, activation patching in GPT-2, and finally sparse autoencoders - a key tool for finding clean features inside messy model activations
author: admin#1742094092

text: Vitalik:EF Is Not Ethereum's Center, but a Smaller Node With a Clear Mission Vitalik Buterin said the Ethereum Foundation is not the "center" of Ethereum, but one node with a clearly defined purpose. He said the EF will become smaller, more long-term-oriented and more opinionated, focusing its limited resources on Ethereum's core priorities, including censorship resistance, capture resistance, openness, privacy and security, rather than broadly taking on ecosystem-building functions. He added that ETH is Ethereum's most financially valuable product, but some of the work needed to support ETH as an asset falls outside the EF's scope and should be carried by other organizations in the ecosystem. Vitalik also disclosed that around 90% of his net worth remains in ETH, while the remaining roughly $40 million in on-chain fiat funds has been fully allocated to open-source research and technology projects. — link

text: The Tell - On Beeple's Cards, Staying Native, and the Tastelessness That Passes for Ethos Beeple launched trading cards -> follows the toy culture and gambling psychology (holographic tiers, pack-ripping dopamine loops) The essay uses Beeple's $10 trading card drop to argue that the NFT ecosystem has largely abandoned artistic substance, critical framework, and genuine community. Instead, it has embraced low-brow "toy culture" and gambling mechanics (holographic rarity tiers, speculative secondary markets) that prioritize short-term market liquidity and dopamine loops over cultural significance While the space brands itself as an open, gatekeeper-free liberation movement, the author asserts that a small group of early institutional artists, platform founders, and mega-collectors maintain strict, traditional consolidation power. Rather than flattening this hierarchy, blockchain transparency merely visualizes it, allowing insider networks to manipulate attention while hiding behind countercult

text: France Accounts for 70% of Global Crypto "Wrench Attacks" According to analyst Joe Nakamoto, approximately 70% of global crypto-related "wrench attacks"—physical extortion of digital assets—are occurring in France. The country has recorded 41 kidnappings so far in 2026, averaging one every 2.5 days. Nakamoto attributes this surge to risks stemming from centralized KYC data breaches. French national prosecutor for organized crime, Vanessa Perrée, confirmed that 88 individuals have been charged in connection with these cases, highlighting a severe escalation in organized violence targeting crypto holders. — link

text: Hyperliquid Has Used Nearly All Trading Fee Revenue, Over $1.16B, to Buy Back HYPE Forbes contributor Zennon Kapron argues that HYPE's recent rally is driven less by ETF expectations than by Hyperliquid's built-in buyback mechanism. Since launch, Hyperliquid has funneled nearly all its trading fee revenue—over $1.16 billion—into open-market HYPE buybacks via its "Assistance Fund." Kapron suggests this quarterly multi-hundred-million-dollar buyback machine provides stronger price support than early ETF inflows. However, he warns the mechanism is volume-dependent: a market downturn could shrink fee revenue, thereby weakening the price floor. (The above is the author's opinion, not investment advice.) — link
